sections.header: Verses 1–6 — "Chosen before the Creation of the World"

heb.paragraph: The cognate forms of eulog- appear three times in v. 3: ‘Blessed (eulogētos) be God who has blessed (eulogēsas) us with every spiritual blessing (eulogia).’ This triadic repetition creates a doxological crescendo. The adjective ‘spiritual’ (pneumatikē) does not mean immaterial but Spirit-mediated: these are blessings conveyed by the Holy Spirit in the heavenly realms.

The verb proorizō (v. 5) denotes God’s prior determination or foreordination. Paul locates this decision ‘before the creation of the world’ (pro katabolēs kosmou, v. 4), establishing that election is grounded in God’s eternal purpose rather than in any foreseen human response. The destination of this predestination is ‘adoption to sonship through Jesus Christ’ (v. 5).

refs.note: Paul’s golden chain of salvation—foreknew, predestined, called, justified, glorified—parallels the sequential unfolding of God’s purpose in Eph 1:3–14, with predestination grounded in God’s prior purpose.

Peter’s opening benediction parallels Ephesians: ‘Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who in his great mercy has given us new birth into a living hope.’ Both letters open with eulogētos formulas celebrating God’s saving initiative.

notes.note: Paul identifies himself as an apostle ‘by the will of God’—a claim consistent with all his prison epistles. The addressees are described as ‘faithful in Christ Jesus,’ emphasising the believers’ union with Christ that will dominate the letter.

Election is ‘in Christ’ and ‘before the creation of the world.’ MacArthur emphasises that God’s choice is unconditional: it precedes human existence and therefore cannot be based on foreseen merit. The purpose of election is ‘to be holy and blameless in his sight.’

Predestination is directed toward a specific end: ‘adoption to sonship.’ The phrase ‘in accordance with his pleasure and will’ locates the ground of election in God’s sovereign delight, not in human worthiness. The goal is doxological: ‘to the praise of his glorious grace.’

Calvin finds in these verses the doctrine of unconditional election stated with maximal clarity. God chose us ‘before the creation of the world’—not because he foresaw that we would believe, but in order to bring us to faith. The phrase ‘in love he predestined us’ (v. 5) shows that God’s love is the cause, not the consequence, of election.

Calvin emphasises the trinitarian structure: the Father elects, the Son redeems, the Spirit seals. Each action terminates in the same refrain: ‘to the praise of his glorious grace’ (v. 6), ‘to the praise of his glory’ (vv. 12, 14). The entire plan exists for God’s glory, not for human benefit, though human benefit flows from it.

The phrase en tois epouraniois (in the heavenly realms) is unique to Ephesians and denotes the transcendent sphere where spiritual realities are operative. The NET translators note that it encompasses both divine blessings (1:3) and hostile powers (6:12).

The prepositional phrase en agapē (in love) at the end of v. 4 / beginning of v. 5 is textually ambiguous: it may modify ‘holy and blameless’ (v. 4) or ‘he predestined’ (v. 5). The NET favours connecting it with ‘predestined,’ making love the motivation for God’s predestining decree.

Lincoln identifies the eulogy (vv. 3–14) as a Jewish berakah adapted for christological purposes. The phrase ‘before the creation of the world’ (pro katabolēs kosmou) locates election in eternity, prior to and independent of the created order. Lincoln notes that the purpose of election (‘holy and blameless’) is both forensic (declared righteous) and ethical (actually transformed), though the emphasis in context falls on the forensic dimension as the ground of the ethical.

O’Brien reads the eulogy as a carefully crafted rhetorical unit in which each phrase builds on the previous one. The repetition of ‘in Christ’ / ‘in him’ / ‘in the One he loves’ (eleven times in vv. 3–14) establishes the christological concentration of the passage. O’Brien emphasises that predestination in v. 5 is to a status (‘adoption to sonship’), not merely to salvation in the abstract. The entire divine purpose is directed toward forming a people who bear the Son’s image and enjoy the Son’s relationship with the Father.

hist.context: Ephesians opens with the longest single sentence in the Greek New Testament: vv. 3–14 form one continuous benediction in the original. This eulogy unfolds God’s saving purpose in trinitarian structure: the Father’s election and predestination (vv. 3–6), the Son’s redemption and revelation of the mystery (vv. 7–12), and the Spirit’s sealing and guarantee of the inheritance (vv. 13–14). The opening section (vv. 1–6) establishes the cosmic scope of God’s plan: believers were chosen ‘in Christ’ before creation, predestined for adoption, and accepted ‘in the One he loves.’ The phrase ‘in the heavenly realms’ (en tois epouraniois, v. 3) is unique to Ephesians (1:3, 20; 2:6; 3:10; 6:12) and designates the spiritual sphere where divine realities are operative.

heb.paragraph: The noun mystērion is one of the key terms in Ephesians (1:9; 3:3, 4, 9; 5:32; 6:19). In the Pauline usage it denotes not an insoluble puzzle but a divine plan formerly hidden and now revealed. The ‘mystery of his will’ (v. 9) is God’s purpose to unite all things in Christ—an eschatological plan that transcends Jewish-Gentile boundaries and encompasses the entire cosmos.

The noun arrabōn (v. 14) is a commercial term denoting a first instalment or guarantee of future payment. The Holy Spirit is the arrabōn of believers’ inheritance—a present, experiential foretaste of the full redemption yet to come. The term occurs also in 2 Cor 1:22 and 5:5, always with reference to the Spirit.

refs.note: Paul’s parallel statement in Colossians—God was pleased to reconcile all things through Christ’s blood—provides the christological basis for the cosmic reconciliation described in Eph 1:10.
Paul’s description of the Spirit as God’s ‘seal’ and ‘deposit guaranteeing what is to come’ uses the same vocabulary as Eph 1:13–14, confirming the consistency of his pneumatology.

notes.note: Redemption is ‘through his blood’—not merely metaphorical but referencing the actual sacrificial death of Christ. The phrase ‘the forgiveness of sins’ defines redemption in terms of release from guilt and penalty. MacArthur emphasises that this redemption is ‘in accordance with the riches of God’s grace,’ not measured by it but flowing from its inexhaustible depth.

The mystery is not esoteric knowledge for an elite few but God’s once-hidden, now-revealed plan to unite all things under Christ’s headship. MacArthur reads ‘the fullness of time’ (tēn oikonomian tou plērōmatos tōn kairōn) as the eschatological consummation toward which all history moves.

The Spirit is both seal (authenticating mark of ownership) and guarantee (first instalment of the coming inheritance). MacArthur notes the trinitarian completeness: the Father planned, the Son accomplished, the Spirit applies and guarantees. The refrain ‘to the praise of his glory’ (v. 14) closes the eulogy with doxology.

Calvin identifies redemption through blood as the centre of the gospel: without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness (Heb 9:22). The mystery of God’s will is the plan to gather all things into one under Christ—a plan so vast that it encompasses the entire cosmos. Calvin marvels at the comprehensiveness: not merely humanity but all things (ta panta) in heaven and on earth.

Calvin explains the Spirit’s sealing as God’s mark of ownership on the believer: just as a seal authenticates a document, the Spirit authenticates the believer’s adoption. The guarantee metaphor adds assurance: the Spirit is the down payment of a full inheritance that is certain to be delivered.

The infinitive anakephalaiōsasthai (to bring together under one head) is a compound of ana (up) and kephalaion (head/summary). The NET translators note that the word implies both recapitulation (summing up) and unification under one head (Christ). The cosmic scope—ta panta, ta en tois ouranois kai ta epi tēs gēs—encompasses the entirety of creation.

The aorist participle esphragisthēte (you were sealed) points to a decisive past event. The articular noun tō arrabōni (the guarantee) indicates the Spirit is the definite, specific guarantee, not one among many.

Lincoln reads the eulogy as structured around three ‘to the praise of his glory’ refrains (vv. 6, 12, 14), marking the trinitarian sections. The mystery (mystērion) is not merely cognitive information but the dynamic unfolding of God’s cosmic purpose. Lincoln notes that the eschatological framework is both already (believers are already ‘in Christ,’ already sealed) and not yet (the inheritance is still awaited, the Spirit is still a ‘deposit’).

O’Brien emphasises that the eulogy’s christological concentration reaches its apex in v. 10: the anakephalaiōsis (summing up) of all things in Christ is the goal toward which the entire divine plan moves. O’Brien notes that this cosmic scope distinguishes Ephesians from all other Pauline letters: salvation is not merely individual or communal but cosmic, encompassing the reconciliation of all created order under Christ’s rule.

hist.context: The eulogy’s second and third movements unfold the Son’s work (vv. 7–12) and the Spirit’s work (vv. 13–14). Redemption through Christ’s blood (v. 7) is the means by which God’s mystery is accomplished: his purpose to ‘bring unity to all things in heaven and on earth under Christ’ (v. 10). The phrase anakephalaiosāsthai ta panta en tō Christō (to sum up all things in Christ) is the thematic centre of the entire letter. The scope is cosmic: not merely individual salvation but the reconciliation of the entire created order under Christ’s headship. The Spirit’s role is to ‘seal’ (sphragizō, v. 13) believers as God’s possession and to serve as the guarantee (arrabōn, v. 14) of the final inheritance.

sections.header: Verses 15–23 — "The Immeasurable Greatness of His Power"

heb.paragraph: Paul accumulates four near-synonyms for power in vv. 19–21: dynamis (power), energeia (working/energy), kratos (might), and ischys (strength). The piling up of power-terms is rhetorical: Paul struggles to express the magnitude of the divine power that raised Christ from the dead and seated him at the Father’s right hand, far above all rule and authority.

refs.note: The hymn’s declaration that God ‘exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name’ parallels Eph 1:20–21, where Christ is seated above all rule and authority.
Paul’s identification of Christ as ‘the head of the body, the church’ provides the parallel to Eph 1:22–23, though Ephesians develops the body-of-Christ ecclesiology more extensively.

notes.note: Paul’s prayer is for experiential knowledge, not mere information. The ‘Spirit of wisdom and revelation’ is the Holy Spirit who illuminates the mind to grasp what God has done. MacArthur notes that the greatest need of the church is not more programmes but more knowledge of God.

The power that raised Christ from the dead is the same power at work in believers. The four power-terms are not synonyms but an escalating series, each adding a dimension of divine might. Christ is seated above all rule, authority, power, and dominion—encompassing every conceivable cosmic hierarchy, present and future.

Christ is appointed head ‘for the church’—his authority over all things serves the church’s benefit. The church as his body receives the fullness of the one who fills the entire cosmos. MacArthur notes the staggering implication: the church is the instrument through which Christ’s cosmic rule is expressed.

Calvin reads the prayer as a petition for the Spirit’s illumination—without which the truths of vv. 3–14 remain mere words. The ‘eyes of your heart’ (v. 18) signifies spiritual perception, not physical sight. Calvin emphasises that God’s power is not abstract but demonstrated in the resurrection: the same power that conquered death now works in believers.

Calvin identifies the headship of Christ over the church as both sovereign authority and organic union. The church is not merely subject to Christ; it is his body, receiving life and direction from him as limbs receive from the head.

The accumulation of power-terms (dynamis, energeia, kratos, ischys) is unparalleled in Paul. The NET translators note that each term adds a nuance: dynamis is inherent power, energeia is operative power, kratos is ruling power, ischys is raw strength.

The phrase to plērōma tou ta panta en pasin plēroumenou (the fullness of him who fills all in all) is grammatically ambiguous. The middle voice plēroumenou could be passive (‘who is filled’) or middle (‘who fills’). The NET favours the active sense: Christ fills all things.

Lincoln reads the prayer as the epistolary counterpart to the eulogy: having praised God for what he has done (vv. 3–14), Paul now prays that the readers will apprehend it (vv. 15–23). The power language of vv. 19–21 is directed against the cosmic powers theme that pervades Ephesians: the same power that raised Christ above all rule and authority is at work in the church. Lincoln argues that ‘the fullness’ (to plērōma) in v. 23 refers to the church as the sphere in which Christ’s presence is fully experienced.

O’Brien notes the prayer’s structure: a thanksgiving (vv. 15–16) followed by a petition (vv. 17–19) that leads to a christological affirmation (vv. 20–23). The exaltation of Christ is described in terms borrowed from Ps 110:1 (‘seated at his right hand’) and Ps 8:6 (‘put all things under his feet’). O’Brien reads the head-body relationship (vv. 22–23) as expressing both authority (headship over) and vitality (organic connection to).

hist.context: Paul’s thanksgiving prayer (vv. 15–23) asks that the Ephesians receive ‘the Spirit of wisdom and revelation’ (v. 17) to know three things: the hope of God’s calling, the riches of his inheritance in the saints, and the immeasurable greatness of his power (vv. 18–19). This power is demonstrated in two arenas: first, in the resurrection and exaltation of Christ above all cosmic authorities (vv. 20–21); second, in the appointment of Christ as head over everything for the church (v. 22), which is his body, the fullness of him who fills all in all (v. 23). The concept of the church as Christ’s body—developed more fully in ch. 4—is introduced here as the climax of the prayer.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Salutation: apostle by God’s will to the saints in Ephesus

rows.label: vv.3–6
rows.text: Eulogy I: the Father’s election and predestination

rows.label: vv.7–12
rows.text: Eulogy II: the Son’s redemption and the mystery revealed

rows.label: vv.13–14
rows.text: Eulogy III: the Spirit’s sealing and guarantee

rows.label: vv.15–19
rows.text: Prayer for knowledge: hope, inheritance, power

rows.label: vv.20–21
rows.text: Christ’s resurrection and exaltation above all powers

rows.label: vv.22–23
rows.text: Christ as head over all for the church, his body

lit.note: Ephesians 1 unfolds in two movements: a trinitarian eulogy (vv. 3–14) celebrating God’s cosmic purpose to unite all things in Christ, and a prayer (vv. 15–23) that believers would apprehend the immeasurable power at work in them. The chapter establishes the letter’s cosmic-ecclesiological framework.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as the chapter celebrates God’s eternal predestination and cosmic authority over all powers. Worship is high through the sustained eulogy form. Faith and Covenant are prominent through the themes of election and adoption.

hebtext.note: The cognate forms of eulog- appear three times in v. 3: ‘Blessed (eulogētos) be God who has blessed (eulogēsas) us with every spiritual blessing (eulogia).’ This triadic repetition creates a doxological crescendo. The adjective ‘spiritual’ (pneumatikē) does not mean immaterial but Spirit-mediated: these are blessings conveyed by the Holy Spirit in the heavenly realms.

The verb proorizō (v. 5) denotes God’s prior determination or foreordination. Paul locates this decision ‘before the creation of the world’ (pro katabolēs kosmou, v. 4), establishing that election is grounded in God’s eternal purpose rather than in any foreseen human response. The destination of this predestination is ‘adoption to sonship through Jesus Christ’ (v. 5).

The noun mystērion is one of the key terms in Ephesians (1:9; 3:3, 4, 9; 5:32; 6:19). In the Pauline usage it denotes not an insoluble puzzle but a divine plan formerly hidden and now revealed. The ‘mystery of his will’ (v. 9) is God’s purpose to unite all things in Christ—an eschatological plan that transcends Jewish-Gentile boundaries and encompasses the entire cosmos.

The noun arrabōn (v. 14) is a commercial term denoting a first instalment or guarantee of future payment. The Holy Spirit is the arrabōn of believers’ inheritance—a present, experiential foretaste of the full redemption yet to come. The term occurs also in 2 Cor 1:22 and 5:5, always with reference to the Spirit.

Paul accumulates four near-synonyms for power in vv. 19–21: dynamis (power), energeia (working/energy), kratos (might), and ischys (strength). The piling up of power-terms is rhetorical: Paul struggles to express the magnitude of the divine power that raised Christ from the dead and seated him at the Father’s right hand, far above all rule and authority.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 1–6) establishes the cosmic scope of God’s plan: believers were chosen ‘in Christ’ before creation, predestined for adoption, and accepted ‘in the One he loves…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Every Spiritual Blessing in Christ

translations.text: For he chose us in him before the creation of the world to be holy and blameless in his sight. In love
even as he chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love
he made known to us the mystery of his will according to his good pleasure, which he purposed in Christ,
making known to us the mystery of his will, according to his purpose, which he set forth in Christ

thread.text: Paul’s golden chain of salvation—foreknew, predestined, called, justified, glorified—parallels the sequential unfolding of God’s purpose in Eph 1:3–14, with predestination grounded in God’s prior purpose.

Peter’s opening benediction parallels Ephesians: ‘Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who in his great mercy has given us new birth into a living hope.’ Both letters open with eulogētos formulas celebrating God’s saving initiati…
Paul’s parallel statement in Colossians—God was pleased to reconcile all things through Christ’s blood—provides the christological basis for the cosmic reconciliation described in Eph 1:10.
Paul’s description of the Spirit as God’s ‘seal’ and ‘deposit guaranteeing what is to come’ uses the same vocabulary as Eph 1:13–14, confirming the consistency of his pneumatology.
The hymn’s declaration that God ‘exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name’ parallels Eph 1:20–21, where Christ is seated above all rule and authority.
Paul’s identification of Christ as ‘the head of the body, the church’ provides the parallel to Eph 1:22–23, though Ephesians develops the body-of-Christ ecclesiology more extensively.

discourse.thesis: God's eternal plan of redemption — predestined in love, accomplished in Christ, sealed by the Spirit — is the ground of all praise and the foundation of Christian identity.

nodes.text: Salutation establishing Paul's identity (apostle by God's will) and the recipients' identity (saints and faithful in Christ Jesus). The grace-peace benediction orients the letter theologically before a word of argument is spoken.

The berakah (blessing) — a single 202-word sentence in Greek, the longest in the NT. Structured as three strophes, each ending in praise 'to the glory of his grace/glory': (1) the Father chooses and predestines in love (vv. 3-6); (2) the Son redeems and reveals the mystery (vv. 7-12); (3) the Spirit seals and guarantees the inheritance (vv. 13-14).

children.text: The Father chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world (πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου) to be holy and blameless. He predestined (προορίσας) us for adoption — the double compound prefix 'pro-' (before/in advance) is emphatic: this is an eternal, prior, divine initiative.

In Christ we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, according to the riches of his grace. God has made known the mystery (μυστήριον) of his will: to unite (ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι — recapitulate/sum up) all things in Christ — things in heaven and on earth.

The Spirit is the seal (σφραγίζω) and down payment (ἀρραβών) of the inheritance until we acquire full possession. The Spirit's presence is eschatological evidence — the 'already' of a 'not yet' complete redemption.

nodes.text: Paul's intercessory prayer — not a general wish but a specific petition shaped by the theology of vv. 3-14. He prays that the Ephesians receive a spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of God, and that the eyes of their hearts be enlightened to know three things.

children.text: The three things: (1) the hope of his calling; (2) the riches of his glorious inheritance in the saints; (3) the immeasurable greatness of his power toward believers — the same power he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead.

The power at work for believers is the resurrection/exaltation power: Christ seated above all rule and authority and power and dominion, all things under his feet, given as head over all things to the church — which is his body, the fullness of the one who fills all in all. The cosmic scope of Christ's lordship grounds the church's identity.

discourse.note: Ephesians 1 is architecturally shaped by trinitarian movement: Father (election, vv. 3-6), Son (redemption, vv. 7-12), Spirit (sealing, vv. 13-14), followed by a prayer that the readers grasp these realities. The berakah form (vv. 3-14) is liturgical — Paul is teaching theology through doxology. Key theological terms: 'chosen' (eklektos), 'predestined' (proorizo), 'mystery' (mysterion), 'fullness' (pleroma).

coaching.tip: The opening blessing (vv. 3-14) is one sentence in Greek — a cascade of divine initiative: 'he chose us... predestined us... lavished on us... made known to us.' Human response barely appears until verse 13 ('you also'). Paul establishes that salvation is God's work before any talk of human responsibility. Theology shapes experience.

'In Christ' or 'in him' appears 11 times in the first chapter. This phrase is Paul's shorthand for union with Christ — we're chosen in him, redeemed in him, sealed in him. All spiritual blessing comes through incorporation into Christ. The Christian life isn't imitation from outside but participation from inside.

Paul prays for the Ephesians to have 'the Spirit of wisdom and of revelation' (v. 17) — not new information but illumination of what God has already done. He prays for 'the eyes of your hearts enlightened' (v. 18). Spiritual perception is a gift to be asked for, not an achievement to be earned. Even understanding grace requires grace.
